strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
3

The warm-up, much like stretching, has an almost sacrosanct reputation in the fitness
community. "It must be done." Yet the need to warm up for any activity poses an
evolutionary conundrum, as illustrated by the following quote.

It may seem like a trivial critique of something so commonly established as warming up
before exercise, but it's a legitimate question: how does it make evolutionary sense that
humans need to warm up before an activity to prevent injury or perform well? It's not
very good for your survival if you pull a hamstring every time you have to make a run for
it in the wild or you dislocate your shoulder every time you throw a spear without having
done your shoulder mobility drills beforehand.

Why should we warm up?
There is a very good theoretical reason to warm up before your workouts. This reason
is… it warms you up.

Warming up your body can increase performance and reduce injury risk via several
mechanisms: physiologically, metabolically and neurally.
• Warm muscles are less viscous. Less viscous muscles are more elastic and offer
less passive resistance to changes in muscle length. This can increase power
and rate of force development in particular, as well as total force output.
Columbus: You should actually limber up as well. Especially if we're going down
that hill. It is very important.

Tallahassee: I don't believe in it. You ever see a lion limber up before it takes
down a gazelle?
-
Zombieland (2009)

4

• During inactivity, the number of bonds between actin and myosin filaments in
muscle tissue increases. As a result, the muscle stiffens. Full range of motion
exercise breaks these bonds and thus reduce the passive resistance of muscle
to changes in length, again potentially increasing power and strength.
• Like most chemical reactions, glycolysis, the breakdown of muscle glycogen to
form energy for your muscle contractions, is sped up by temperature. Warm
muscles can thus produce more energy.
• Warm tissue has a higher nerve conduction velocity, allowing faster neural
signaling. Signals from the brain are thus received by your muscles more quickly
and vice versa. This particularly increases your reaction times and rate of force
development.
• In warm muscles, the bond between oxygen and hemoglobin is weaker. This
makes it easier for the hemoglobin in your red blood cells to release oxygen to
your muscle tissue.

• An increase in muscle temperature causes the blood vessels to widen
(vasodilation), again making it easier for the body to deliver oxygen from the
lungs via the blood to your muscles.
• Active warm-ups increase baseline oxygen consumption, providing a buffer
against oxygen deprivation (hypoxia) during anaerobic exercise like strength
training.

5

In ancestral times in the wild, our warm-ups were likely largely biochemical: fear, anger
and other forms of arousal can give us an internal shot of adrenaline and cortisol that
serves to get our temperature and heart rate up. Without such excitement, we have to
rely on more mechanical methods to warm ourselves up before our artificial workouts.

While we have an impressive list of theoretical benefits attained by increasing muscle
and core body temperature, research finds only inconsistent benefits of warming up.

Studies on the effects of warming up
Abad et al. (2011) found support for the conventional warm-up structure of a general
aerobic warm-up followed by exercise-specific submaximal sets before you start your
work sets. Specifically, they found that the 1RM leg press strength of trained men was
higher after the addition of a 20-minute warm-up on a bicycle than after only performing
2 light sets of leg pressing before their work sets. This study supports the general
recommendation to perform a general warm-up to raise core body temperature and then
a specific warm-up for each exercise to practice the movement pattern and potentially
increase muscle temperature further.

Barroso et al. (2013) further supported the results of Abad et al. and even found a dose-
response effect of the warm-up intensiveness on 1RM leg press strength in trained
men: see the graph below. 5 Minutes of cycling was not enough to significantly increase
strength, but 20 minutes of low-intensity cycling (40% of VO2max) was, resulting in an
average of 3% more strength. 20 minutes of moderate intensity cycling (70% of
VO2max) proved to be overkill, as it decreased performance. The excessive warm-up
was also perceived as significantly effortful based on the trainees' heart rates and
perceived exertion, so your intuition should be a good guide on what's enough and
what's excessive. Your warm-up shouldn't result in significant fatigue. Other research in
swimmers also supports that excessively 'warming up' can decrease performance and,
headings: Studies on the effects of warming up

pages.text:
6

in contrast to this study, Van Den Tillaar et al. (2016) found that more than 10 minutes
of warming up is excessive for soccer players.

Mean (+SD) leg-press 1RM values for each GWU condition
*: p≤0.05 compared to CTRL (control), SDMI (short duration moderate intensity), SDLI
(short duration low intensity), and LDLI (long duration, low intensity)
†: p≤0.05 compared to CTRL, SDMI, SDLI, and LDMI.

However, other research finds minimal effects of warming up for submaximal strength
tests, regardless of how you do it. Ribeiro et al. (2014) compared the effects of 4
different warm-up protocols on strength training performance (4 sets of bench presses,
squats, and arm curls to failure at 80% of 1RM) in novice level trained men.
• Control: the lay-back-on-the-exercise-mat-while-pretending-to-exercise-and-staring-
at-the-cardio-bunnies for 10 minutes protocol.
• Specific warm-up: a set of 10 reps with 50% of 1RM before each exercise.
• General aerobic warm-up: 10 minutes of low intensity cycling (which somehow
achieved 40 km/hr).
• Combined warm-up: a combination of the aerobic and the specific warm-up.
The result: zero difference in total repetitions performed or work on the bench press, the
squat or biceps curls. Warming up, regardless of how, did not improve strength training

7

performance. One caveat is that this study took place in Brazil, where, simply walking to
the gym may be enough to increase your body temperature significantly due to the
generally warm climate. Pre-workout heat therapy can also improve strength training
performance without requiring any activity, while pre-workout cryotherapy reduces
strength training performance without inducing any neuromuscular fatigue. So you may
need more warming up in colder training environments.

However, Barnes et al. (2016) again found insignificant effects of general warm-ups on
peak power output during the high-pull exercise in trained men. The warm-ups
consisted of either bicycling or whole body vibration, the effects of which were very
similar. A specific warm-up of submaximal high pulls did improve performance, but
adding a general warm-up did not cause any further improvement, even though it did
increase muscle temperature. The specific high-pull warm-up in this study consisted of
4 sets of 5 reps up to 85% of their working weights, so it's plausible that this relatively
extensive warm-up made a general warm-up redundant. Still, this study indicates an
exercise-specific warm-up is likely more important for strength trainees than a general
warm-up, at least for technical exercises.

Olympic high pulls with a sumo stance

Takizawa et al. (2018) found no effect of treadmill warm-ups, regardless of intensity, on
time to exhaustion in track athletes.

8

Konrad et al. (2022) found no effects on hamstring strength or jump height of adding an
intensive dynamic stretching warm-up to a foam rolling session, regardless of order.

Indirect evidence suggests warming up may reduce muscle damage from training
compared to not warming up. A 2022 meta-analysis by Tan et al. found that pre-workout
vibration training reduced muscle soreness and markers of muscle damage in the days
after the workout. There's no established mechanism by which vibration would achieve
this and the control groups often had a sham or no intervention, so it's likely that this
protective effect was a result of being more warmed up. Cold muscles are more
sensitive to straining.

Overall, warming up does not seem to have very consistent effects on performance, but
theory and some data do suggest benefits of both a general warm-up and especially a
specific warm-up.

Performance aside, warm-ups could still improve our gains by increasing muscle
activity. A common theory is that warming up induces neuromuscular potentiation.

Neuromuscular potentiation
Warming up is sometimes hypothesized to potentiate the nervous system to achieve
better motor control or reach higher levels of muscle activation. Let's look at what the
research says about this theory.

One particularly interesting line of research is in violinists. Yes, people that play the
musical instrument called the violin. McCrary et al. (2016) studied the effects of warming
up in violinists as a model for exercise technique. Playing the violin is an exceedingly
technical form of exercise requiring fine motor control, much more so than the
Powerlifts, for example. At the elite level it's also far more demanding than you might

9

expect, requiring heart rates over 72% of the predicted maximum during a performance.
As such, any neural benefits of warming up for exercise are likely to be particularly
evident in violinists. Yet even in violinists, warming up did not affect muscle activity
patterns or musical performance. It did decrease the ratings of perceived exertion
(RPEs) of the violinists during their performance, suggesting that most benefits of
warming up are psychological and not physical.

Barnes et al. (2016) also found no effect of a general warm-up, a specific warm-up or
both on muscle activity during Olympic high-pulls. It thus appears that muscular control
does not require a warm-up, not even during technical exercises. Our brains are
perfectly capable of directing our muscles without a warm-up.

Multiple studies have also specifically researched the effects of incorporating 'glute
activation drills' into the warm-up, generally not finding any relevant effects on
performance or muscle activity.
• Nadel (2013) studied the effect of warming up on muscle activation. He
specifically investigated the often-heard claim of 'gluteal amnesia' that people
can't intentionally contract their glutes well without prior activation work. He found
that adding gluteus medius activation drills to a general warm-up with dynamic
stretching didn't affect muscle activation patterns during glute training in strength-
trained individuals.
• Parr et al. (2017) also found no beneficial effect of glute activation drills on actual
gluteus maximum activation during hang pulls, an explosive Olympic weightlifting
exercise, in professional rugby players. There was also no increase in ground
reaction force. In fact, peak gluteus maximus activation decreased, yet there
was a trend for a greater contribution in force production of the glutes and
hamstrings, indicating the exercise was performed more hip-dominantly after the
glute activation. This could be interpreted as efficient in that there was more force
production in the glutes with less muscle activation, but since it didn't improve
total force production, it's more apt to say the biomechanics merely changed, not
headings: reaction force. In fact, peak gluteus maximus activation decreased, yet there

pages.text:
10

improved. It's not necessarily desirable to 'disrupt' the body's intuitive way it
performs a movement. In fact, excessive glute warm-ups may be detrimental to
performance based on other studies.
• Barry et al. (2016) found that extremely extensive glute activation warm-ups
(more like workouts) had no overall significant effect on sprinting speed in rugby
players compared to a control group, but at certain time points, some glute
activation groups were significantly slower. The researchers concluded: "These
results would indicate that performing gluteal warm-up protocols with varying
repetition ranges has a negative effect on post warm-up acceleration
performance."
• Comyns et al. (2015) found that adding glute activation drills to a regular warm-
up decreased jump height in track and field athletes, although ground reaction
forces were not negatively affected at most time points.
• Healy & Harrison (2014) found no effects of adding glute activation drills to a
warm-up on any measure of jumping performance.
• Crow et al. (2012) found that a glute activation warm-up increased peak power
output during Smith machine jumps, but the other groups didn't warm up at all or
did a short whole-body vibration session, which turned out to work no better than
doing nothing at all. As such, the theoretical performance enhancement was
likely just a general warm-up effect rather than any specific effect of glute
activation. Similarly, Pinfold et al. (2018) found that a glute activation warm-up
improved the jumping performance of rugby players compared to 10 minutes of
doing nothing at all. Yet even compared to nothing, sprint times did not improve
and glute muscle activation (EMG) did not increase after the purported glute
activation warm-up.
• Sander et al. (2013) found no effect on sprint performance of adding 'functional
exercises', including glute activation drills, to the general warm-up of soccer
players.
• Harrison & McCabe (2017) again found no consistent performance enhancing
effect of glute activation exercises on sprinting and jumping.
headings: activation groups were significantly slower. The researchers concluded: “These
up decreased jump height in track and field athletes, although ground reaction

pages.text:
11

• Cannon et al. (2022) found that a week of intensive, bi-daily glute training
increased muscle activity during submaximal lower body movements; however,
since the participants were not strength trained, this may have simply been due
to strength gains. Plus, since the testing exercises were done very submaximally,
we don't know if muscle activity increased during intensive training. Many effects
on muscle activity disappear during maximal effort training, as muscle activity
levels are then often maximized regardless (i.e. a ceiling effect).
• Arguably most relevant of all, Cochrane et al. (2017) studied the effect of adding
glute activation drills to the warm-ups of semi-professional rugby players for 6
weeks before their strength training workouts. The glute activation drills did not
enhance the development of glute strength or glute activation (EMG).

All in all, we have strong evidence that activation drills do not actually increase muscle
activation, and warming up may not be necessary altogether to reach maximum muscle
activity.

How about stretching: should we do that in our warm-ups?

Dynamic stretching
Dynamic stretching does tend to improve sports performance and there is a faint trend
for the occasional percent increase in strength training performance as well. We also
know that post-activation potentiation is a real phenomenon (see the course module on
advanced training techniques).

But there is a crucial difference between sports and weight training. In the gym, your
warm-up sets for each exercise are already inherently a form of dynamic stretching,
especially if you're training with a full range of motion (ROM). In contrast, many
explosive athletic movements, like jumping, are by their very nature impossible to
headings: Dynamic stretching does tend to improve sports performance and there is a faint trend

pages.text:
12

perform with a slow speed, and bodyweight movements cannot be performed with a
reduced intensity. Such movements benefit from dynamic stretching beforehand as a
proxy for the type of warm-up sets you perform in the gym. They're a way to exercise
the used musculature with full ROM in a somewhat explosive manner.

In the gym, however, we have the luxury of being able to do the most specific possible
warm-up imaginable: we can simply do the exact exercise we're going to do but with
less weight. Performing warm-up sets with progressively more weight before your work
sets makes (further) stretching redundant for strength training.

If we look at the list of mechanisms by which body temperature improves performance,
it makes perfect sense that sports benefit far more from an extensive warm-up than
strength training does. Most sport activities are far more reliant on rate of force
development (jumping, throwing, sprinting, etc.), glycolysis and oxygen delivery than
pure strength training.

In short, exercise-specific warm-ups generally make dynamic stretching redundant for
strength trainees.

Static stretching
Static stretching tends to decrease force and power production. Static stretching can
decrease muscle activation levels and consequently force production, because it
essentially prepares the nervous system to relax the muscle, not to activate it. As such,
you should generally actively avoid static stretching in your warm-ups. See the course
module on stretching for more details.

Practical application: how to warm up
headings: Static stretching tends to decrease force and power production. Static stretching can
Practical application: how to warm up

pages.text:
13

The old-school warm-up structure with a general aerobic warm-up to get warm followed
by exercise-specific submaximal sets before your work sets has withstood the test of
time. Start with 5-20 minutes of low-intensity cardio on an incline treadmill (brisk
walking: no jogging), rower, Stairmaster, bike ergometer or elliptical/cross-trainer. You
can stop when you feel an increase in body temperature. Most people shouldn't need
more than 10 minutes of total general warm-up time, but you can decide the exact
warm-up duration by feel.

Then before each exercise's work sets, perform 1-3 very submaximal sets with
progressively heavier weights. Begin with no weight and move up in weight towards
your work set in 1 to 3 sets of 3 to 12 reps. The higher the weight and the more
technical the exercise, the more sets you'll need to warm up and the lower the reps on
your warm-up sets should be. For example, for a technical compound exercise like
squats you'll likely need at least 3 sets of 5 reps with progressively more weight before
you perform your first work set. For simple isolation exercises, a single set of 12 reps
with about half of your working weight is often enough. If you have no idea what weights
to select, do the following warm-up sets based on your estimated 1RM: 60% x 8, 70% x
5, 80% x 3. Don't fatigue yourself during your warm-up. Focus on technique and a
smooth, explosive movement. The goal of the warm-up is to be able to perform your
work sets safely with full ROM and maximal performance.

Research does not support the use of additional stretching or muscle activation drills.

Since the benefits of warming up are likely largely mediated by temperature, you should
be able to shorten your warm-up by wearing warm clothing. If you're already sweaty or
feeling very warm, you probably only need minimal warming up. Again, you can largely
go by feel here.

There's no need to overcomplicate your general warm-up beyond this, but it doesn't hurt
to throw in some dynamic stretching to be safe, especially for muscle groups with a lot

14

of three-dimensional range of motion like the hips and shoulders, since dynamic
stretching is in itself an effective way to increase your body temperature. Previously
injured body parts may also benefit from more warming up. Here's an example of a very
safe general warm-up. In home gyms or warm gyms, you could do this instead of cardio
as your general warm-up. In cold settings, you may want to do both.
• Some bodyweight Romanian deadlifts, dumbbell goblet squats, (knee) push-ups
and (unilateral) glute bridges.
• Scapular wall slides x 5 (try to increase ROM). After a few sessions, you can take
these out of your warm-up, unless you feel they still help increase your shoulder
stability.
• Shoulder dislocations x 10 with (long) towel. Do the movement slowly. Don't
force it. You can skip these if they don't feel right.

15

Cooling down
You may be wondering about the flipside of the warm-up, the 'cool-down'. This concept
seems to have no better rationale than the intuition that if you need to warm up, you
should also cool down. While making some vague intuitive sense, the need to cool
down completely flies in the face of logic and common sense.

First, any activity you do will increase thermogenesis and the fastest way to cool down
is thus always to remain passive.

Second, the body is a master of homeostasis. It cools down perfectly fine on its own
without requiring any special intervention. Even more so than warming up, cooling down
makes no evolutionary sense.

In conclusion, cool-down routines do not improve training outcomes or reduce injury
risk.

16

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Explain why warmer muscles can achieve better exercise performance than cold
muscles.
• Explain why dynamic stretching may improve sport performance but not strength
training performance.
• Explain why you don't need a cool-down routine after strength training.
• Prescribe effective warm-up protocols for a given strength training program.

➢ Click here to try a mock exam for this module

10

improved. It's not necessarily desirable to 'disrupt' the body's intuitive way it
performs a movement. In fact, excessive glute warm-ups may be detrimental to
performance based on other studies.
• Barry et al. (2016) found that extremely extensive glute activation warm-ups
(more like workouts) had no overall significant effect on sprinting speed in rugby
players compared to a control group, but at certain time points, some glute
activation groups were significantly slower. The researchers concluded: "These
results would indicate that performing gluteal warm-up protocols with varying
repetition ranges has a negative effect on post warm-up acceleration
performance."
• Comyns et al. (2015) found that adding glute activation drills to a regular warm-
up decreased jump height in track and field athletes, although ground reaction
forces were not negatively affected at most time points.
• Healy & Harrison (2014) found no effects of adding glute activation drills to a
warm-up on any measure of jumping performance.
• Crow et al. (2012) found that a glute activation warm-up increased peak power
output during Smith machine jumps, but the other groups didn't warm up at all or
did a short whole-body vibration session, which turned out to work no better than
doing nothing at all. As such, the theoretical performance enhancement was
likely just a general warm-up effect rather than any specific effect of glute
activation. Similarly, Pinfold et al. (2018) found that a glute activation warm-up
improved the jumping performance of rugby players compared to 10 minutes of
doing nothing at all. Yet even compared to nothing, sprint times did not improve
and glute muscle activation (EMG) did not increase after the purported glute
activation warm-up.
• Sander et al. (2013) found no effect on sprint performance of adding 'functional
exercises', including glute activation drills, to the general warm-up of soccer
players.
• Harrison & McCabe (2017) again found no consistent performance enhancing
effect of glute activation exercises on sprinting and jumping.


--- Page 11 ---
11

• Cannon et al. (2022) found that a week of intensive, bi-daily glute training
increased muscle activity during submaximal lower body movements; however,
since the participants were not strength trained, this may have simply been due
to strength gains. Plus, since the testing exercises were done very submaximally,
we don't know if muscle activity increased during intensive training. Many effects
on muscle activity disappear during maximal effort training, as muscle activity
levels are then often maximized regardless (i.e. a ceiling effect).
• Arguably most relevant of all, Cochrane et al. (2017) studied the effect of adding
glute activation drills to the warm-ups of semi-professional rugby players for 6
weeks before their strength training workouts. The glute activation drills did not
enhance the development of glute strength or glute activation (EMG).

All in all, we have strong evidence that activation drills do not actually increase muscle
activation, and warming up may not be necessary altogether to reach maximum muscle
activity.

How about stretching: should we do that in our warm-ups?

Dynamic stretching
Dynamic stretching does tend to improve sports performance and there is a faint trend
for the occasional percent increase in strength training performance as well. We also
know that post-activation potentiation is a real phenomenon (see the course module on
advanced training techniques).

But there is a crucial difference between sports and weight training. In the gym, your
warm-up sets for each exercise are already inherently a form of dynamic stretching,
especially if you're training with a full range of motion (ROM). In contrast, many
explosive athletic movements, like jumping, are by their very nature impossible to


--- Page 12 ---
12

perform with a slow speed, and bodyweight movements cannot be performed with a
reduced intensity. Such movements benefit from dynamic stretching beforehand as a
proxy for the type of warm-up sets you perform in the gym. They're a way to exercise
the used musculature with full ROM in a somewhat explosive manner.

In the gym, however, we have the luxury of being able to do the most specific possible
warm-up imaginable: we can simply do the exact exercise we're going to do but with
less weight. Performing warm-up sets with progressively more weight before your work
sets makes (further) stretching redundant for strength training.

If we look at the list of mechanisms by which body temperature improves performance,
it makes perfect sense that sports benefit far more from an extensive warm-up than
strength training does. Most sport activities are far more reliant on rate of force
development (jumping, throwing, sprinting, etc.), glycolysis and oxygen delivery than
pure strength training.

In short, exercise-specific warm-ups generally make dynamic stretching redundant for
strength trainees.

Static stretching
Static stretching tends to decrease force and power production. Static stretching can
decrease muscle activation levels and consequently force production, because it
essentially prepares the nervous system to relax the muscle, not to activate it. As such,
you should generally actively avoid static stretching in your warm-ups. See the course
module on stretching for more details.

Practical application: how to warm up


--- Page 13 ---
13

The old-school warm-up structure with a general aerobic warm-up to get warm followed
by exercise-specific submaximal sets before your work sets has withstood the test of
time. Start with 5-20 minutes of low-intensity cardio on an incline treadmill (brisk
walking: no jogging), rower, Stairmaster, bike ergometer or elliptical/cross-trainer. You
can stop when you feel an increase in body temperature. Most people shouldn't need
more than 10 minutes of total general warm-up time, but you can decide the exact
warm-up duration by feel.

Then before each exercise's work sets, perform 1-3 very submaximal sets with
progressively heavier weights. Begin with no weight and move up in weight towards
your work set in 1 to 3 sets of 3 to 12 reps. The higher the weight and the more
technical the exercise, the more sets you'll need to warm up and the lower the reps on
your warm-up sets should be. For example, for a technical compound exercise like
squats you'll likely need at least 3 sets of 5 reps with progressively more weight before
you perform your first work set. For simple isolation exercises, a single set of 12 reps
with about half of your working weight is often enough. If you have no idea what weights
to select, do the following warm-up sets based on your estimated 1RM: 60% x 8, 70% x
5, 80% x 3. Don't fatigue yourself during your warm-up. Focus on technique and a
smooth, explosive movement. The goal of the warm-up is to be able to perform your
work sets safely with full ROM and maximal performance.

Research does not support the use of additional stretching or muscle activation drills.

Since the benefits of warming up are likely largely mediated by temperature, you should
be able to shorten your warm-up by wearing warm clothing. If you're already sweaty or
feeling very warm, you probably only need minimal warming up. Again, you can largely
go by feel here.

There's no need to overcomplicate your general warm-up beyond this, but it doesn't hurt
to throw in some dynamic stretching to be safe, especially for muscle groups with a lot


--- Page 14 ---
14

of three-dimensional range of motion like the hips and shoulders, since dynamic
stretching is in itself an effective way to increase your body temperature. Previously
injured body parts may also benefit from more warming up. Here's an example of a very
safe general warm-up. In home gyms or warm gyms, you could do this instead of cardio
as your general warm-up. In cold settings, you may want to do both.
• Some bodyweight Romanian deadlifts, dumbbell goblet squats, (knee) push-ups
and (unilateral) glute bridges.
• Scapular wall slides x 5 (try to increase ROM). After a few sessions, you can take
these out of your warm-up, unless you feel they still help increase your shoulder
stability.
• Shoulder dislocations x 10 with (long) towel. Do the movement slowly. Don't
force it. You can skip these if they don't feel right.


--- Page 15 ---
15

Cooling down
You may be wondering about the flipside of the warm-up, the 'cool-down'. This concept
seems to have no better rationale than the intuition that if you need to warm up, you
should also cool down. While making some vague intuitive sense, the need to cool
down completely flies in the face of logic and common sense.

First, any activity you do will increase thermogenesis and the fastest way to cool down
is thus always to remain passive.

Second, the body is a master of homeostasis. It cools down perfectly fine on its own
without requiring any special intervention. Even more so than warming up, cooling down
makes no evolutionary sense.

In conclusion, cool-down routines do not improve training outcomes or reduce injury
risk.


--- Page 16 ---
16

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Explain why warmer muscles can achieve better exercise performance than cold
muscles.
• Explain why dynamic stretching may improve sport performance but not strength
training performance.
• Explain why you don't need a cool-down routine after strength training.
• Prescribe effective warm-up protocols for a given strength training program.
